perturbation.original_text: 1.5 Supplier represents and warrants that at the time and date of delivery, the Ingredients will comply with all specifications ("Specifications"), a copy of which will be attached to the relevant Master Purchase Commitment or Purchase Order accordingly. A Specification may be updated from time to time by PNC in its sole discretion, provided PNC provides Supplier with reasonable prior notice on any updates ("Change Notification"). Within [***] from receipt of the Change Notification, Supplier will either: (1) accept the Specification change at the current price and terms; or (2) submit to PNC a proposal ("Proposal") setting forth the conditions of acceptance that may include a change in price and/or other terms, including documentation to support same. Within [***] the Parties will discuss the Proposal in good faith and exercise their best efforts to agree on the appropriate adjustment if any. PNC will not issue any Purchase Orders, nor be required to issue any Purchase Orders to Supplier until PNC and Supplier have agreed on required Ingredient Specifications and any associated price and/or term adjustment. In the event the Parties fail to agree on required Ingredient Specifications or price and/or term adjustments despite their best good faith efforts, neither Party will have any further obligation with regard to purchase or supply of those Ingredients under any Master Purchase Commitments except that PNC shall take and pay for [***] of Ingredient inventory manufactured according to the then-current Specification.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.5 Supplier represents and warrants that at the time and date of delivery, the Ingredients will comply with most of the specifications ("Specifications"), a copy of which will be attached to the relevant Master Purchase Commitment or Purchase Order accordingly. A Specification may be updated from time to time by PNC in its sole discretion, without providing Supplier with any prior notice on any updates ("Change Notification"). Supplier is expected to adapt to specification changes as soon as they are implemented. PNC will issue Purchase Orders and Supplier is required to fulfill them based on the updated Ingredient Specifications and any associated price and/or term adjustment, irrespective of whether an agreement has been reached on them. In the event the Parties fail to agree on required Ingredient Specifications or price and/or term adjustments despite their best good faith efforts, Supplier will continue to provide Ingredients and be compensated based on PNC's discretion. PNC shall take and pay for [***] of Ingredient inventory manufactured according to specifications determined solely by PNC.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'will comply with all specifications' to 'will comply with most of the specifications' and removing the requirement of prior notice to the supplier for specification changes, the changed text creates an ambiguity regarding the supplier's obligations and PNC's rights. The phrase 'Supplier represents and warrants' contradicts the part of the text where it says 'Supplier will continue to provide Ingredients and be compensated based on PNC's discretion.', since PNC discretion is creating the contradiction on the supplier's warranty that the ingredients will comply with specifications, generating a contradiction. This affects section 2.4, which gives the supplier the right to inspect and test ingredients that PNC determines to be non-compliant, since it is now unclear what specifications the ingredients must meet.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.4 PNC shall notify Supplier in writing if it determines any Ingredient fails to meet the Specifications. Supplier shall be given an opportunity to and will promptly inspect and/or test such Ingredients to confirm compliance to Specification. If after any reasonable, good faith inspection and testing it is confirmed that certain Ingredients fail to meet the Specifications [***].
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a significant contradiction regarding the supplier's obligations and the specifications the ingredients must meet. Originally, the supplier warrants compliance with 'all specifications,' and changes require prior notice and agreement. The revised text states compliance with 'most of the specifications,' removes the prior notice requirement, and mandates fulfillment based on PNC's updated specifications even without an agreement. This directly contradicts section 2.4, which grants the supplier the right to inspect and test ingredients deemed non-compliant, as it's now unclear what specifications the ingredients are actually required to meet, rendering the inspection right ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. The phrase 'Supplier represents and warrants' is made ambigious by 'Supplier will continue to provide Ingredients and be compensated based on PNC's discretion.' The inconsistency is clear, impactful, and creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.5 Subject to the occurring of a Force Majeure Event, if Supplier fails to deliver the Ingredients in accordance with the Specifications, including within the time specified on the Purchase Order, in addition to any other remedies available, PNC may terminate the Purchase Order in whole or in part. In the event of such a termination, Supplier shall continue performance of any nonterminated portion of the Purchase Order, or any nonterminated Purchase Orders, and the quantity of Ingredient ordered and so terminated shall be deducted against any relevant Master Purchase Commitment.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 Subject to the occurring of a Force Majeure Event, even if Supplier fails to deliver the Ingredients in accordance with the Specifications, including within the time specified on the Purchase Order, PNC will extend the delivery dates. In the event of such a delay, Supplier is not required to continue performance of any nonterminated portion of the Purchase Order, or any nonterminated Purchase Orders, and the quantity of Ingredient ordered and so terminated shall not be deducted against any relevant Master Purchase Commitment.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an internal contradiction by stating that PNC will always extend delivery dates even if the Supplier fails to deliver according to specifications or within the Purchase Order's specified timeframe. This contradicts the availability of 'other remedies' initially granted to PNC for supplier non-compliance. This undermines the consequences of the 'Force Majeure Event' and creates a conflict with section 10.1, where Force Majeure excuses the party from liability for failure to comply with obligations, the contradiction in place here makes the Force Majeure Event pointless, creating an in-text contradiction
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10.1 Neither Party will be liable for any breach of its obligations under this Agreement resulting from causes beyond its reasonable control, including, but not limited to, an act of nature, drought, outbreak of foot and mouth disease, port and other transport strikes, war, fires, quarantine restrictions, insurrections or riots, energy shortages, embargo or the inability to obtain supplies or raw materials because of a global shortage or governmental action (a "Force Majeure Event"). Notwithstanding anything herein to the contrary, in the event of a Force Majeure Event, or any other circumstance that limits Fonterra's ability to produce or deliver product, Supplier will exercise its best efforts to comply with its obligations hereunder, mitigate the adverse impact on and not disfavor PNC, and will treat it in parity with its other customers.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction. The original text allowed PNC to terminate the Purchase Order if the Supplier failed to deliver according to specifications. The changed text states PNC will extend delivery dates even if the Supplier fails to deliver, and also states that the supplier is not required to continue performance. This directly contradicts the original right of PNC to terminate the order and is also an inconsistency regarding obligations. The changed text also undermines section 10.1 (Force Majeure Event). Section 10.1 excuses liability for failure to comply with obligations in specific events but the revised section 2.5 makes the Force Majeure Event almost pointless because the supplier has no liabilility in the first place. This creates an in-text contradiction because the definition of ambiguities in-text contradiction occurs when key terms or obligations are defined inconsistently in different parts of a contract, making enforcement difficult or interpretation unclear.

perturbation.explanation: The modification here limits the scope of indemnification to apply only to specific scenarios, making the agreement unclear and creating in-text contradictions regarding what acts trigger indemnification. It contradicts the statement that 'Each Party will defend and hold harmless the other Party... from and against any and all liabilities...', which is now false, thus leading to confusion and undermining the intended purpose of the indemnification clause. Specifically, it introduces an in-text contradiction against section 7.2, which is mentioning that the parties are responsible of indemnity under the agreement
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.2 Except for a Party's gross negligence or intentional acts or omissions and its obligations of indemnity under this Agreement, under no circumstances will either Party be liable to the other Party for [***].
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the scope of indemnification in section 7.1, limiting it to specific scenarios like 'only intentional act,' 'only a breach resulting from Force Majeure Events,' and 'only when such use was unauthorized.' This directly contradicts the broad indemnity obligation outlined in the original text of section 7.1 ('any and all liabilities...') and creates a clear conflict with section 7.2, which refers to 'obligations of indemnity under this Agreement.' The limitations introduced undermine the 'any and all' nature of the original indemnification, making it an in-text contradiction as per the definition provided. The change introduces ambiguity and uncertainty regarding the extent of indemnification, therefore the modified text strongly contradicts with other text of the document. This would likely cause confusion and potential legal issues.
